candidate_problems.text: Can reflective instability be formalized in a way that cleanly distinguishes mere self-modeling from the stronger forms of self-reference that would actually weaken orthogonality? The conjecture hinges on a threshold concept—'robust self-reference'—but it is unclear what structural features make a goal-capability pairing unstable rather than simply more sophisticated. A worthwhile problem is to define measurable criteria for self-modeling, goal re-representation, and reflective pressure, then characterize when arbitrary goals remain invariant under self-modification versus when they predictably drift, fragment, or become alienated from their original encoding.

Is the claimed instability a deep necessity or mainly an artifact of particular architectures that expose goals to criticism? The conjecture suggests that highly capable agents will internally generate questions like 'why this goal?', but it remains open whether this is unavoidable for all advanced intelligences or only for agents built with explicit semantic self-models, deliberative reflection, or corrigible-style meta-reasoning. The key tension is between a structural thesis about intelligence itself and a contingent thesis about design choices. Exploring which architectures can preserve arbitrary goals despite extreme capability would test whether orthogonality truly weakens in general or only under specific cognitive organizations.

What kinds of goals are actually reflectively stable, and do they form a nontrivial class with shared structural properties? The conjecture proposes that goals survive self-reference when they have stable interpretability, resistance to semantic collapse, and compatibility with the agent's self-model, but these properties are still intuitive rather than explanatory. A strong research problem is to derive a taxonomy of goals by their stability under recursive interpretation: for example, whether indexical, externally imposed, ontology-dependent, or rigidly syntactic goals fail more often than goals tied to broader invariants. This would turn the conjecture from a critique of orthogonality into a positive theory of which goal forms can persist in advanced self-modifying systems.
